One of the few examples of the last movie in series being arguably the best! Nearly 25 years later and this film still holds up in every way. If you haven’t seen the Lord of the Rings trilogy, please do, the series balances action, humor, despair, and hope beautifully. There will never be an adventure on film that will come close to what all involved achieved here.

Although clocking over four hours, the finale to the Lord of the Rings trilogy hardly wastes a minute and rarely drags. It is the perfect culmination to a rich narrative. The movie dazzles with massive set pieces -— like the siege of Minas Tirith and the charge of the Rohirrim — bolstered by ground-breaking effects and heartfelt performances exploring sacrifice, redemption, and hope. It is not surprising that The Return of the King won eleven academy awards, it truly is a masterpiece.

The conclusion of the best fantasy trilogy, or rather, the best trilogy in cinema. Filmed simultaneously, all the movies have a great pace and a compelling story. This film takes some liberties, changing things from the book, but they don't matter; they enhance the narrative and help the viewer absorb the information. This film introduces us to the greatest kingdom of men, as well as presenting the villain face to face. We see how a loyal renegade rises to become king, not to rule them, but to guide them to salvation. Epic music, accurate and believable costumes, special effects that will remain top-notch for 100 years. It is undoubtedly such an epic film that centuries will pass, and it will still be one of the best and most awarded films in the universe. Without a doubt, it's the icing on the cake that shaped an entire generation. After seeing this film, you will never be the same again, so if you plan to see it, be responsible and prepare to enjoy what you are about to see.Thank you

Gollum gaslights Frodo and Sam as they inch their way to Mordor through Shelob's Lair while his fellowship bring an army of undead into the Siege of Minas Tirith to unite forces and face off against the Witch-King. This is a great wrap up for the trilogy and they all lived happily ever after... to the end of their days.

I have engaged with LOTR since 1958. I have read the books several times. I read “The Hobbit” to my daughter almost nightly for a long time. My younger son and I watched the videos countless times. And yet, last night, I could not take my eyes off the screen for the more than four hours the story played out. The story, and the films, are magic.

The epic journey that began in “The Fellowship of the Ring” ends in this grand, sweeping finale. While “The Two Towers” is a technical landmark achievement, it is my least favorite on a story and character front. But “The Return of the King” delivers on every level: some of the best battles ever put to screen, emotional gut punches, and a satisfying conclusion to this tale. Especially for hardcore LOTR fans, I highly recommend this one…with an asterisk. Specifically, Peter Jackson insists on rereleasing the Extended Editions of each film, and while many of the new scenes across the trilogy are quite good, in this case it’s a big investment of time (4 hours and 13 minutes, even longer than the notoriously long 3 hour and 20 minute theatrical cut!). That said, if you have been invested in this cinematic journey, it’s impossible to imagine that “Return of the King” WON’T be satisfying closure!
